constructs.definition: A style of reasoning that privileges quantitative metrics as having jurisdiction over political judgments, collapses heterogeneous social complexity into a single aggregated trajectory, and then inscribes the result in portable artifacts that travel as if they were neutral facts. It is the use of numerical abstractions to close political questions before they can be opened, to present simplifications as comprehensiveness, and to frame dissent as a failure to understand the data. Extending Theodore Porter's account of mechanical objectivity as a technology of trust, it operates through three orientations — the will to simplify, the rule of expertise, and the politics of inevitability — and is the epistemic infrastructure that lebensraum imaginaries require.

A recurring epistemic architecture within ecofascism: an ensemble of future narratives, organizational practices, and rhetorical repertoires through which ecological emergency is converted into demographic hierarchy and coercive governance is presented as reluctant but responsible management of natural limits. Lebensraum is a deliberate reference to a concept crossing biological, ecological, social, and political boundaries that not only animated the calamities of the Third Reich but has a longer history and more unsettling afterlives. An imaginary is a collectively held structure that makes certain actions seem necessary, certain populations seem threatening, and certain policies seem like responsible concessions — one that individuals, organizations, data, and arguments enter and exit even as the structure persists and adapts. Extending Griffin's account of palingenetic ultranationalism, it specifies how ecological crisis supplies the rebirth myth and quantitative instruments supply the epistemic infrastructure; the book reserves 'ecofascism' for the verdict on individuals.

chapters.title: Preface
chapters.teaser: Not a verdict on individuals but a tracing of the structures that recruit them.
chapters.pitch: An archival encounter, the naming of the two operating concepts — quantitative chauvinism and lebensraum imaginaries — and a politically direct closing.
chapters.summary: The preface names the book's two distinct but co-evolving concepts. Quantitative chauvinism provides the epistemic infrastructure that lebensraum imaginaries require: the simplified models, credentialed expertise, and foreclosure of alternatives without which naturalizing demographic hierarchy loses its scientific alibi. It distinguishes the analytic target — lebensraum imaginaries as a recurring architecture — from a verdict on any individual, reserving 'ecofascism' for the verdict, and frames inoculation as anticipatory recognition of the moves by which numerical claims become moral permission structures.

chapters.title: Boulder
chapters.teaser: A progressive city builds the most elaborate growth-control machinery in America — and ends up whiter than the state around it.
chapters.pitch: Boulder, Colorado: a nominally progressive community where conservation slid gradually into exclusion, each step looking like responsible stewardship from the inside.
chapters.summary: Boulder introduces the book's two operating concepts through a hundred years of its own institutional history: the Colorado Chautauqua of 1898, the Olmsted Brothers commission of 1908–1910, the blue line drawn to stop the city climbing into the mountains and the greenbelt drawn to stop it spreading into the plains, and the carrying-capacity ordinances that limited population growth in the name of environmental protection. A nominally progressive community built one of the most elaborate growth-management apparatuses in the country — and produced a city that was, by 2020, 83 percent non-Hispanic white in a state two-thirds so. Calculations about population growth, density, and carrying capacity became judgments about who belongs without ever requiring justification beyond the numbers themselves. The slide from conservation into exclusion was gradual enough that each step looked, from the inside, like responsible stewardship.

chapters.title: The Inheritance
chapters.teaser: Before Bartlett ever picked up the chalk, the apparatus, the vocabulary, and the audience were already in place.
chapters.pitch: The eugenics apparatus, the German science of Lebensraum, the conservation movement, and the postwar population-concern community — the formation Bartlett's lecture entered, already assembled.
chapters.summary: Bartlett's lecture was not an original act of creation; it entered a formation assembled across the first half of the twentieth century. Four currents converge: the institutional eugenics apparatus Davenport and Laughlin built at the Eugenics Record Office at Cold Spring Harbor (1910–1940), codified into sterilization law and ratified in Buck v. Bell; the German biogeographical tradition of Lebensraum (Friedrich Ratzel to Johan Rudolf Kjellén to Karl Haushofer); the American conservation movement, sharing donors and personnel with eugenics through Madison Grant, Henry Fairfield Osborn, and the Boone and Crockett Club; and the postwar population-concern community — the Population Council, the Hugh Moore Fund, Paul Ehrlich, Garrett Hardin's lifeboat ethics, and the Club of Rome. The argument is not that the community was consciously eugenicist but that it inherited the earlier formation's epistemic infrastructure: the categories it counted with, the questions it asked, the institutions it trusted. It is here that the book first locates quantitative chauvinism in its earliest form — in Davenport's eugenic ledger of projected future costs, a privileged number granted jurisdiction over a political question, the projection treated as a finding rather than a choice.

chapters.title: The Bottle
chapters.teaser: He gave the same lecture 1,742 times. Each time, arithmetic quietly became obligation.
chapters.pitch: Bartlett's bacteria-in-a-bottle lecture turned exponential arithmetic into environmental common sense — the chapter names the mechanism: domesticated scientism.
chapters.summary: Albert Bartlett — a Manhattan Project physicist who had measured plutonium isotopes at Los Alamos — delivered the same lecture, 'Arithmetic, Population, and Energy,' more than 1,742 times between 1969 and his late eighties, with the same transparencies and the same dramatic pauses. Its core was an arithmetic trick: divide seventy by a percentage growth rate to approximate its doubling time, dramatized through bacteria doubling in a bottle that is still only half full one minute before noon. In under five minutes the demonstration did three things — it proved exponential compounding as mathematics, produced the visceral shock of a finite container filling all at once, and embedded a moral (stop growth before the bottle fills) that, as Bartlett never acknowledged and the tutorial form ensured his audiences never examined, did not actually follow from the first two. Between 1968 and 1994 the population-concern community converted this exponential arithmetic and ecological carrying capacity into environmental 'common sense' about overpopulation, and then into a program for controlling who could live where. The chapter answers its era question — how did population anxiety become environmental common sense? — by naming the mechanism domesticated scientism: the process by which expert claims, stripped of their methodological caveats and disciplinary contexts, are re-presented as civic fact through pedagogies that disguise the political work the claims are doing.

chapters.title: The Quarantine
chapters.teaser: After Cairo, the movement lost its license — so it built a new one out of footnotes.
chapters.pitch: With fertility control delegitimized after Cairo, immigration restriction became the residual lever, laundered through the aesthetics of neutral policy expertise.
chapters.summary: In September 1994 the Cairo International Conference on Population and Development — the achievement of a decade-long transnational feminist coalition — replaced population stabilization with reproductive health as a human right, centering women's autonomy rather than aggregate fertility rates. For those who still held that exponential growth was a physical law rather than a political choice, Cairo was a delegitimation; but the conviction that population was the master variable of ecological crisis did not vanish, it migrated. Between 1994 and 2008 it moved from international development into domestic immigration restriction: a network of organizations (the Federation for American Immigration Reform, the Center for Immigration Studies, NumbersUSA) that John Tanton built into a parallel credentialing apparatus — think tanks resembling policy shops, reports resembling academic research, spokespeople resembling disinterested experts — which laundered restrictionist conclusions through the aesthetics of neutral expertise, with the numbers doing the laundering. The era's name runs in two directions at once: mainstream institutions quarantined populationism (Cairo, the Sierra Club's expulsion of the population-immigration frame, the Southern Poverty Law Center's hate-group designation), while the network's own instruments — the fiscal-burden table, the environmental impact statement, the cultural-carrying-capacity argument — treated immigration as contamination requiring demographic quarantine. Beneath the formally race-neutral fiscal and ecological vocabulary ran the lebensraum imaginary Chapter 2 traced to its origins, now in an administrative key. The chapter answers a double-edged era question — how did these ideas survive their own delegitimation, and shed their association with racism in the process? — and calls the method agnotological infrastructuring.

chapters.title: The Swarm
chapters.teaser: A federal press release became one of the most-cited numbers in a mass-shooter's manifesto.
chapters.pitch: The 2008 Census crossover chart traveled, stripped of its caveats, from a federal agency into the 'great replacement' narrative.
chapters.summary: On August 14, 2008, the Census Bureau released projections showing the non-Hispanic white population falling below fifty percent in 2042 — eight years sooner than its prior estimate — accompanied by a chart of two converging lines. The projection was conditional; the chart was not. Media coverage stripped the conditionality, conflated 'white' with 'non-Hispanic white' (the total white population was projected to stay above seventy percent), and converted a probabilistic scenario into a declarative countdown. Experimental work by Craig and Richeson showed that mere exposure to the majority-minority framing shifted white Americans toward group-status threat and conservative identification — the chart did not just describe a future, it helped produce one. Stripped of caveats by screenshots and memes, the crossover chart traveled from a federal agency to chan-boards to replacement narratives and into manifestos. Federal demographers, doing competent demographic science within their mandate and the racial categories OMB Directive 15 required, had produced exactly the object restrictionists could never have manufactured themselves. The chapter calls the operation demographic engineering.

chapters.title: The Portfolio
chapters.teaser: Overpopulation panic flips into its mirror image: where did all the babies go?
chapters.pitch: Pronatalism recruits the same quantitative infrastructure as zero population growth: wombs as policy levers, births as fiscal returns.
chapters.summary: As replacement anxiety crested, the headline inverted — from 'minorities will be the majority' to 'where have all the babies gone?' Pronatalism recruited the very same quantitative infrastructure: fiscal-burden analysis became demographic return-on-investment, and wombs became policy levers. A 2024 legal complaint challenging the FDA's approval of mifepristone arrived at the Supreme Court with an actuarial appendix that treated each prevented birth as a fiscal loss and each birth as a return, extending the demographic reasoning Dobbs had planted in reproductive jurisprudence. The chapter develops two concepts — fine-tuning (financial optimization applied to demography) and temporal gerrymandering (manipulating time horizons to manufacture urgency) — and shows that zero population growth and pronatalism are structurally the same project. In the brief's fertility tables the will to simplify, the rule of expertise, and the politics of inevitability operate within a single document.

chapters.title: The Evacuation
chapters.teaser: When the bottle is finally full, the question stops being who stays and becomes who gets rescued.
chapters.pitch: A speculative final era where cascading climate catastrophe turns demographic sorting into automated triage, and a bioregional vocabulary becomes the idiom in which Hardin's lifeboat returns.
chapters.summary: The final era is speculative; its mechanisms are not. Cascading climate catastrophe shifts the governing question from who belongs to who gets rescued. The instrument is no longer a curve but a model of the shocks themselves — shock-spillover modeling, traced from The Limits to Growth (1972) through the climate-security literature and the IPCC's Sixth Assessment (2022), in which a disruption in one region is forecast as a cascade carried by the displaced into the regions that receive them. The framework's distinctive move is to make the receiving region's absorption capacity the central governance variable. The chapter follows that apparatus to an autonomous decision system issuing evacuation determinations faster than any human review, and shows how a bioregional, restorative vocabulary — regenerative sovereignty — becomes the progressive idiom in which Hardin's lifeboat returns.

chapters.title: The Long Term
chapters.teaser: The future has been ceded to people who use it as a warrant for present cruelty. Take it back.
chapters.pitch: Reclaiming long-term thinking from longtermisms — drawing intersectional longtermism from communities that have already survived long-term projects of erasure.
chapters.summary: Long-term thinking has been surrendered to longtermisms that treat the future as a license for present coercion — the effective-altruism / TESCREAL constellation that converts the future into a quantity to be maximized — a lineage Gebru and Torres trace back to the same eugenic traditions this book documents — and the technocratic long term of discount rates and integrated assessment modeling. This chapter reclaims it — drawing intersectional longtermism from five traditions that have already survived long-term projects of erasure and had to think in generations to do it: Black abolition and environmental justice, Indigenous continuance and decolonial ecology, disability justice, feminist political economy and reproductive justice, and antifascist-cooperative organizing. From them it distills a constructive program — quantitative egalitarianism, which demotes the number from verdict to evidence, and solidarity infrastructuring, the work of carrying these traditions across generations into the long heat.

chapters.title: Boulder Again
chapters.teaser: What the model strips from bacteria — memory, feedback, survival — is what lebensraum imaginaries strip from us.
chapters.pitch: The book returns to Boulder's ground — Sand Creek, deep time, and the question of what the valley owes the people in a warming world.
chapters.summary: Opening on the two obituaries that followed Bartlett's 2013 death — a beloved educator and civic leader in the Daily Camera, a movement hero in The Social Contract, Tanton's white-nationalist organ — the book returns to where it opened. Whether Bartlett himself was an ecofascist is set aside as the uninteresting question. Bartlett's bacteria-in-a-bottle is read as a micro-macro isomorphism: what the model deletes from real bacteria — feedback, coordination, memory (CRISPR-Cas, quorum sensing), and survival — is exactly what lebensraum imaginaries delete from human communities, and the five resistance traditions of Chapter 8 are the macro counter-case to that macro simplification. The chapter brings the argument down to Boulder's own ground — the Cheyenne and Arapaho, the Sand Creek Massacre as the local precedent for what the architecture produces under stress, and a deep-time future of water, fire, and heat — and turns the nativist question of who belongs into the question of what the valley owes the people in a rapidly warming world.
